Numerical Linear Algebra

The Gram–Schmidt Algorithm and QR Factorization

The Gram–Schmidt Algorithm and QR Factorization

The previous chapter closed with a lemma about orthogonal projectors: if QQQ has orthonormal columns, then P=QQTP = QQ^{\mathsf T}P=QQT projects any vector onto R(Q)R(Q)R(Q). That lemma was stated for a QQQ we already had in hand. This chapter answers the natural next question — given an arbitrary linearly independent set of vectors, how do we actually build such a QQQ? The answer is the Gram–Schmidt algorithm, and it turns out to be nothing more than repeated application of the projector idea: take a new vector, project out everything already covered by the vectors built so far, and normalize whatever remains. Carrying this out on the columns of a matrix AAA produces exactly the QRQRQR factorization promised back in our very first list of matrix factorizations — and, as we’ll see, QRQRQR gives us a numerically superior alternative to [[LU Factorization|LULULU]] for both solving square systems and tackling least-squares problems.

Objective and Orthogonal Projection

Given a linearly independent set B={x1,x2,…,xn}B = \{x_1, x_2, \dots, x_n\}B={x1​,x2​,…,xn​} in an inner-product space (e.g., Rm\mathbb{R}^mRm with ⟨y,z⟩=yTz\langle y,z\rangle = y^{\mathsf T}z⟨y,z⟩=yTz), construct an orthonormal set U={u1,u2,…,un}U = \{u_1, u_2, \dots, u_n\}U={u1​,u2​,…,un​} with Span⁡(U)=Span⁡(B)\operatorname{Span}(U) = \operatorname{Span}(B)Span(U)=Span(B).

The tool we’ll lean on throughout is the Fourier-expansion projection formula from the previous chapter, restated here for a general orthonormal set rather than a full basis.

Orthogonal Projection (Fourier Expansion)

If {u1,…,uk}\{u_1, \dots, u_k\}{u1​,…,uk​} is an orthonormal set, then for any xxx,

proj⁡span⁡{u1,…,uk}(x)=∑i=1k⟨ui,x⟩ui.\operatorname{proj}_{\operatorname{span}\{u_1,\dots,u_k\}}(x) = \sum_{i=1}^k \langle u_i, x\rangle u_i.projspan{u1​,…,uk​}​(x)=i=1∑k​⟨ui​,x⟩ui​.

Classical Gram–Schmidt: Inductive Construction

The construction proceeds one vector at a time: normalize the first vector to get u1u_1u1​, then repeatedly strip away the part of each new xkx_kxk​ that already lies in the span of the uiu_iui​‘s built so far, and normalize what’s left.

Base Case

u1=x1∥x1∥.u_1 = \frac{x_1}{\|x_1\|}.u1​=∥x1​∥x1​​.

Inductive Step

Suppose u1,…,uk−1u_1, \dots, u_{k-1}u1​,…,uk−1​ are orthonormal and Span⁡{u1,…,uk−1}=Span⁡{x1,…,xk−1}\operatorname{Span}\{u_1,\dots,u_{k-1}\} = \operatorname{Span}\{x_1,\dots,x_{k-1}\}Span{u1​,…,uk−1​}=Span{x1​,…,xk−1​}. Define

vk=xk−∑i=1k−1⟨ui,xk⟩ui,uk=vk∥vk∥(assuming vk≠0).v_k = x_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} \langle u_i, x_k\rangle u_i, \qquad u_k = \frac{v_k}{\|v_k\|} \quad (\text{assuming } v_k \ne 0).vk​=xk​−i=1∑k−1​⟨ui​,xk​⟩ui​,uk​=∥vk​∥vk​​(assuming vk​=0).

[!tip] Interpretation

  • vkv_kvk​ is xkx_kxk​ with its components along u1,…,uk−1u_1,\dots,u_{k-1}u1​,…,uk−1​ removed — the part of xkx_kxk​ orthogonal to everything built so far.
  • If vk=0⃗v_k = \vec 0vk​=0, then xkx_kxk​ was already inside Span⁡{x1,…,xk−1}\operatorname{Span}\{x_1,\dots,x_{k-1}\}Span{x1​,…,xk−1​}, contradicting linear independence of BBB.

This process is what actually delivers on the objective stated above — it’s worth confirming that it does.

Gram–Schmidt Orthonormalization

Given a linearly independent set {x1,…,xn}\{x_1,\dots,x_n\}{x1​,…,xn​}, the construction above produces an orthonormal set {u1,…,un}\{u_1,\dots,u_n\}{u1​,…,un​} with Span⁡{u1,…,uk}=Span⁡{x1,…,xk}\operatorname{Span}\{u_1,\dots,u_k\} = \operatorname{Span}\{x_1,\dots,x_k\}Span{u1​,…,uk​}=Span{x1​,…,xk​} for every kkk.

Proof Sketch

The base case is immediate. For the inductive step, vkv_kvk​ is orthogonal to each uiu_iui​ (i<ki<ki<k) by construction, and normalizing gives ∥uk∥=1\|u_k\| = 1∥uk​∥=1. Span equality holds because xk=vk+∑i=1k−1⟨ui,xk⟩uix_k = v_k + \sum_{i=1}^{k-1}\langle u_i,x_k\rangle u_ixk​=vk​+∑i=1k−1​⟨ui​,xk​⟩ui​, and vk≠0v_k \ne 0vk​=0 precisely when xkx_kxk​ contributes a genuinely new direction.

Matrix Notation and Projectors

Writing the same construction in matrix form connects it directly back to the projector lemma from the previous chapter. Let Uk−1=[u1  u2  ⋯  uk−1]∈Rm×(k−1)U_{k-1} = [u_1 \; u_2 \; \cdots \; u_{k-1}] \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times(k-1)}Uk−1​=[u1​u2​⋯uk−1​]∈Rm×(k−1). Then:

  • Coefficients:  r1:(k−1), k=Uk−1Txk\ r_{1:(k-1),\,k} = U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T}x_k r1:(k−1),k​=Uk−1T​xk​.
  • Projection onto Span⁡{u1,…,uk−1}\operatorname{Span}\{u_1,\dots,u_{k-1}\}Span{u1​,…,uk−1​}:  Uk−1(Uk−1Txk)=∑i=1k−1⟨ui,xk⟩ui\ U_{k-1}\big(U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T}x_k\big) = \sum_{i=1}^{k-1}\langle u_i,x_k\rangle u_i Uk−1​(Uk−1T​xk​)=∑i=1k−1​⟨ui​,xk​⟩ui​.
  • Orthogonal component and normalization:
vk=(I−Uk−1Uk−1T)xk,uk=vk∥vk∥.v_k = (I - U_{k-1}U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T})x_k, \qquad u_k = \frac{v_k}{\|v_k\|}.vk​=(I−Uk−1​Uk−1T​)xk​,uk​=∥vk​∥vk​​.
Interpretation

Uk−1Uk−1TU_{k-1}U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T}Uk−1​Uk−1T​ is exactly the orthogonal projector P=QQTP = QQ^{\mathsf T}P=QQT from the previous chapter’s lemma, applied to Q=Uk−1Q = U_{k-1}Q=Uk−1​. Subtracting it off — as in I−Uk−1Uk−1TI - U_{k-1}U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T}I−Uk−1​Uk−1T​ — projects onto the orthogonal complement of Span⁡{u1,…,uk−1}\operatorname{Span}\{u_1,\dots,u_{k-1}\}Span{u1​,…,uk−1​}, which is exactly the “remove what’s already covered” step Gram–Schmidt needs at every iteration.

Classical Gram–Schmidt (CGS): The Algorithm

Packaging the construction as pseudocode makes the sequence of operations explicit:

Input: linearly independent vectors x1,…,xn∈Rmx_1,\dots,x_n \in \mathbb{R}^mx1​,…,xn​∈Rm.

For k=1k = 1k=1 to nnn:

  1. vk←xkv_k \leftarrow x_kvk​←xk​
  2. For i=1i = 1i=1 to k−1k-1k−1: compute rik←uiTxkr_{ik} \leftarrow u_i^{\mathsf T}x_krik​←uiT​xk​
  3. vk←xk−∑i=1k−1rik uiv_k \leftarrow x_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} r_{ik}\,u_ivk​←xk​−∑i=1k−1​rik​ui​
  4. rkk←∥vk∥r_{kk} \leftarrow \|v_k\|rkk​←∥vk​∥
  5. uk←vk/rkku_k \leftarrow v_k / r_{kk}uk​←vk​/rkk​
Numerical Stability

Classical Gram–Schmidt can suffer a loss of orthogonality in finite-precision arithmetic, because every rikr_{ik}rik​ in step 2 is computed against the original xkx_kxk​ rather than an updated residual. We’ll return to this issue — and its fix — after building the QRQRQR factorization itself.

QR Factorization via Gram–Schmidt

Applying Gram–Schmidt to the columns of a matrix, rather than an abstract vector set, produces one of the fundamental factorizations introduced back at the start of the course.

Let A=[a1  a2  ⋯  an]∈Rm×nA = [a_1\; a_2\; \cdots\; a_n] \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}A=[a1​a2​⋯an​]∈Rm×n with m≥nm \ge nm≥n and linearly independent columns.

QR Factorization (Full Column Rank)

There exist Q∈Rm×nQ \in \mathbb{R}^{m\times n}Q∈Rm×n with orthonormal columns (QTQ=InQ^{\mathsf T}Q = I_nQTQ=In​) and R∈Rn×nR \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}R∈Rn×n upper triangular with positive diagonal, such that

A=QR.A = QR.A=QR.

The factorization is unique once we require RRR‘s diagonal entries to be positive.

The construction is exactly Classical Gram–Schmidt applied to the columns of AAA, with the coefficients rikr_{ik}rik​ collected directly into the matrix RRR:

  • q1=a1/∥a1∥q_1 = a_1 / \|a_1\|q1​=a1​/∥a1​∥.
  • For k=2,…,nk = 2,\dots,nk=2,…,n:
    • rik=qiTakr_{ik} = q_i^{\mathsf T}a_krik​=qiT​ak​ for i=1,…,k−1i = 1,\dots,k-1i=1,…,k−1,
    • vk=ak−∑i=1k−1rik qiv_k = a_k - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} r_{ik}\,q_ivk​=ak​−∑i=1k−1​rik​qi​,
    • rkk=∥vk∥>0r_{kk} = \|v_k\| > 0rkk​=∥vk​∥>0,
    • qk=vk/rkkq_k = v_k / r_{kk}qk​=vk​/rkk​.

In matrix form,

A=QR,Q=[q1  q2  ⋯  qn],R=[r11r12⋯r1n0r22⋯r2n⋮⋱⋱⋮0⋯0rnn],rij=qiTaj  (i≤j).A = QR, \qquad Q = [q_1\;q_2\;\cdots\;q_n], \qquad R = \begin{bmatrix} r_{11} & r_{12} & \cdots & r_{1n} \\ 0 & r_{22} & \cdots & r_{2n} \\ \vdots & \ddots & \ddots & \vdots \\ 0 & \cdots & 0 & r_{nn} \end{bmatrix}, \qquad r_{ij} = q_i^{\mathsf T}a_j \ \ (i \le j).A=QR,Q=[q1​q2​⋯qn​],R=​r11​0⋮0​r12​r22​⋱⋯​⋯⋯⋱0​r1n​r2n​⋮rnn​​​,rij​=qiT​aj​  (i≤j).
Interpretation

For each kkk, column aka_kak​ decomposes as ak=∑i=1krik qia_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} r_{ik}\,q_iak​=∑i=1k​rik​qi​ — a direct readout of the fact that Span⁡{q1,…,qk}=Span⁡{a1,…,ak}\operatorname{Span}\{q_1,\dots,q_k\} = \operatorname{Span}\{a_1,\dots,a_k\}Span{q1​,…,qk​}=Span{a1​,…,ak​}. Requiring rkk>0r_{kk} > 0rkk​>0 pins down the sign ambiguity in each qkq_kqk​, which is exactly what makes the factorization unique.

Solving Systems with QR

With A=QRA = QRA=QR in hand, solving linear systems reduces to a single triangular solve — and the same factorization handles both square systems and overdetermined least-squares problems.

Square, nonsingular A∈Rn×nA \in \mathbb{R}^{n\times n}A∈Rn×n:

Ax=b  ⟹  QRx=b.Ax = b \implies QRx = b.Ax=b⟹QRx=b.

Left-multiplying by QTQ^{\mathsf T}QT and using QTQ=IQ^{\mathsf T}Q = IQTQ=I gives Rx=QTbRx = Q^{\mathsf T}bRx=QTb, an upper-triangular system solved directly by back substitution.

Least squares, overdetermined (m≥nm \ge nm≥n, full column rank): The goal is to minimize ∥Ax−b∥\|Ax - b\|∥Ax−b∥. Since QQQ has orthonormal columns, it preserves length (from the previous chapter), so

∥Ax−b∥=∥QT(Ax−b)∥=∥Rx−QTb∥.\|Ax - b\| = \|Q^{\mathsf T}(Ax-b)\| = \|Rx - Q^{\mathsf T}b\|.∥Ax−b∥=∥QT(Ax−b)∥=∥Rx−QTb∥.

Minimizing this reduces to solving Rx=QTbRx = Q^{\mathsf T}bRx=QTb — again by back substitution.

QR vs. LU: A Quick Comparison

TopicQRLU (without pivoting)
ExistenceFor AAA with linearly independent columns (m≥nm \ge nm≥n)For square AAA; may require pivoting
ShapeA=Q (m×n)  R (n×n)A = Q\,(m\times n)\; R\,(n\times n)A=Q(m×n)R(n×n), RRR upper triangularA=LUA = LUA=LU, LLL lower, UUU upper (square AAA)
UniquenessUnique if diag⁡(R)>0\operatorname{diag}(R) > 0diag(R)>0Not unique (depends on pivoting/scaling)
Works for rectangular AAAYes (full column rank)No (typically square only)
Solve Ax=bAx=bAx=b (square AAA)One back substitution (Rx=QTbRx = Q^{\mathsf T}bRx=QTb)Forward (Ly=bLy=bLy=b) + back (Ux=yUx=yUx=y)
Least squares (overdetermined)Yes, natural via QTQ^{\mathsf T}QTNot standard

Modified Gram–Schmidt (MGS)

We flagged earlier that Classical Gram–Schmidt can lose orthogonality in floating-point arithmetic. The fix is a small but important reordering of the same computation: instead of computing every coefficient rikr_{ik}rik​ against the original xkx_kxk​, each subtraction updates vkv_kvk​ immediately and the next coefficient is computed against that already-updated residual.

Algorithm:

For k=1k = 1k=1 to nnn:

  1. vk←akv_k \leftarrow a_kvk​←ak​
  2. For i=1i = 1i=1 to k−1k-1k−1:
    • rik←qiTvkr_{ik} \leftarrow q_i^{\mathsf T}v_krik​←qiT​vk​
    • vk←vk−rik qiv_k \leftarrow v_k - r_{ik}\,q_ivk​←vk​−rik​qi​
  3. rkk←∥vk∥r_{kk} \leftarrow \|v_k\|rkk​←∥vk​∥
  4. qk←vk/rkkq_k \leftarrow v_k / r_{kk}qk​←vk​/rkk​
Why This Helps

Because each coefficient in MGS is computed against the current residual vkv_kvk​ rather than the original aka_kak​, small rounding errors from earlier subtractions get accounted for immediately, rather than compounding silently until the end. In exact arithmetic, CGS and MGS compute identical results — the difference is purely about how errors propagate under floating-point rounding.

Projector Viewpoint for MGS

The claim that CGS and MGS agree in exact arithmetic is easiest to see through the projector language from earlier in this chapter. Define the rank-one projector onto the orthogonal complement of a unit vector uiu_iui​:

Ei=I−uiuiT,∥ui∥=1.E_i = I - u_i u_i^{\mathsf T}, \qquad \|u_i\| = 1.Ei​=I−ui​uiT​,∥ui​∥=1.

Applying EiE_iEi​ to a vector removes its component along uiu_iui​. Chaining these projectors — which is what MGS does, one step at a time — gives

vk=(Ek−1Ek−2⋯E1) xk.v_k = \big(E_{k-1}E_{k-2}\cdots E_1\big)\,x_k.vk​=(Ek−1​Ek−2​⋯E1​)xk​.
Product of Complement Projectors

If u1,…,uk−1u_1,\dots,u_{k-1}u1​,…,uk−1​ are mutually orthonormal, then

Ek−1Ek−2⋯E1=I−∑i=1k−1uiuiT.E_{k-1}E_{k-2}\cdots E_1 = I - \sum_{i=1}^{k-1} u_iu_i^{\mathsf T}.Ek−1​Ek−2​⋯E1​=I−i=1∑k−1​ui​uiT​.
Proof

Expanding a single pair of factors,

(I−ujujT)(I−uiuiT)=I−uiuiT−ujujT+(ujujT)(uiuiT).(I - u_ju_j^{\mathsf T})(I - u_iu_i^{\mathsf T}) = I - u_iu_i^{\mathsf T} - u_ju_j^{\mathsf T} + (u_ju_j^{\mathsf T})(u_iu_i^{\mathsf T}).(I−uj​ujT​)(I−ui​uiT​)=I−ui​uiT​−uj​ujT​+(uj​ujT​)(ui​uiT​).

The cross term vanishes: (ujujT)(uiuiT)=uj(ujTui)uiT=0(u_ju_j^{\mathsf T})(u_iu_i^{\mathsf T}) = u_j(u_j^{\mathsf T}u_i)u_i^{\mathsf T} = 0(uj​ujT​)(ui​uiT​)=uj​(ujT​ui​)uiT​=0 for i≠ji\ne ji=j, since ujTui=0u_j^{\mathsf T}u_i = 0ujT​ui​=0 by orthonormality. Every mixed term in the full product cancels the same way, and induction over kkk gives the stated identity. ■\blacksquare■

This is exactly what reconciles the projector view with the matrix-notation section earlier: with Uk−1=[u1  ⋯  uk−1]U_{k-1} = [u_1\;\cdots\;u_{k-1}]Uk−1​=[u1​⋯uk−1​],

Ek−1⋯E1=I−Uk−1Uk−1T,vk=(I−Uk−1Uk−1T) xk,uk=vk∥vk∥.E_{k-1}\cdots E_1 = I - U_{k-1}U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T}, \qquad v_k = (I - U_{k-1}U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T})\,x_k, \qquad u_k = \frac{v_k}{\|v_k\|}.Ek−1​⋯E1​=I−Uk−1​Uk−1T​,vk​=(I−Uk−1​Uk−1T​)xk​,uk​=∥vk​∥vk​​.

Removing components one at a time (MGS) and removing the entire projection at once (CGS) are, mathematically, the same operation — the lemma is exactly why. The two algorithms only diverge once rounding error enters the picture.

Summary: Key Identities

  • Projection onto Span⁡{u1,…,uk−1}\operatorname{Span}\{u_1,\dots,u_{k-1}\}Span{u1​,…,uk−1​}: proj⁡span⁡(xk)=Uk−1Uk−1Txk.\operatorname{proj}_{\operatorname{span}}(x_k) = U_{k-1}U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T}x_k.projspan​(xk​)=Uk−1​Uk−1T​xk​.
  • Orthogonal component: vk=(I−Uk−1Uk−1T)xk.v_k = (I - U_{k-1}U_{k-1}^{\mathsf T})x_k.vk​=(I−Uk−1​Uk−1T​)xk​.
  • Normalization: uk=vk∥vk∥.u_k = \frac{v_k}{\|v_k\|}.uk​=∥vk​∥vk​​.
  • QR entries: rik=qiTak  (i≤k),rkk=∥vk∥>0,A=QR.r_{ik} = q_i^{\mathsf T}a_k \ \ (i\le k), \qquad r_{kk} = \|v_k\| > 0, \qquad A = QR.rik​=qiT​ak​  (i≤k),rkk​=∥vk​∥>0,A=QR.
Practical Notes
  • A full-column-rank QRQRQR requires m≥nm \ge nm≥n and linearly independent columns of AAA.
  • Enforcing rkk>0r_{kk} > 0rkk​>0 is what makes (Q,R)(Q,R)(Q,R) unique.
  • In practice, prefer Modified Gram–Schmidt — or, better still, Householder QRQRQR — over Classical Gram–Schmidt for numerical stability.
